job_title: Data Science Methods and Statistical Learning
department: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering
application_procedure: To apply, please go to: https://tams.fase.utoronto.ca/TamsPortal/postings/MIE
duties: Duties may include, but are not limited to: • Grading assignments, tests, and/or project work • Supporting course-related administrative tasks • Consultation with the instructor as required • Responding to student inquiries, as needed
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: • Advanced understanding of statistical inference and machine learning • Substantial research experience in data science and/or machine learning • Full proficiency in data science programming in Python, and proficiency in one or more data science frameworks such as PyTorch or Stan • Current or incoming graduate student (SGS) preferred • Demonstrated academic strength in the relevant subject area • Prior TA or teaching experience is an asset • Strong computational or programming skills (as applicable to course content) • Ability to evaluate project-based or implementation-heavy work
salery: $53.92 per hour + vacation pay. Should rates stipulated in the Collective Agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the Collective Agreement shall prevail.

department: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering
application_procedure: Please submit a Unit 1 Course Instructor Application Form, Resume and Teaching Dossier (merged into a single PDF file ) to the MIE Undergraduate Office by email at ugservices@mie.utoronto.ca no later than June 2, 2026 at 11:59PM (Eastern Time). The Course Instructor Application Form can be found on the MIE Careers page here: https://www.mie.utoronto.ca/faculty-staff/careers/. Applicants must include full contact information of their supervisor, plus two U of T employees (faculty or staff) who can testify to the teaching skills of the applicant.  The appointment will be made at the earliest possible time before the commencement of classes by the Associate Chair (Undergraduate) of the Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering.  No other offers or notices of the outcome of applications are authorized by the Department.  Final availability of the position is contingent upon final course determination, enrolment, budgetary considerations, and the final determination of assignments flowing from Article 14:03 of the Collective Agreement.
duties: Preparation of lectures and course materials; delivery of lectures; supervision of Teaching Assistants; setting and marking of tests and exams; evaluation of final grades; contact with students.
experience: Past teaching experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: Applicants should have a strong record of presenting lectures or acting as a teaching assistant.  Applicants must be able to demonstrate considerable depth of knowledge and experience in the subject area.  All applicants should have excellent communication skills - both oral and written. Professional Engineer (P.Eng.) or Limited License (LEL) is strongly preferred. Engineering Intern (EIT) is also acceptable.
qualifications_preferred: N/A

application_procedure: https://utapcsc.utm.utoronto.ca
duties: Must be available for grading the deferred exams. All TAs will be responsible for grading deferred exams. Prompt completion of grading with tight turnaround times (within the specifications of the Collective Agreement, and as discussed with the course coordinator), is a requirement for this role.
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Excellence in CSC343H5 or equivalent. Strong knowledge of SQL, relational algebra, and relational design theory (functional dependencies, normal forms etc). Experience in PostgreSQL and MySQL/MariaDB, DB transactions, and concurrency control. Experience with map reduce, big data analytics, and data visualization is an asset. Expertise with the CSC343H5 content, and beyond. Strong grades in a minimum of 3.0 FCEs of CSC courses from UTM, including at least 1.0 FCEs of systems courses. Note that CSC-like courses offered in different departments do not count towards the FCE requirement as the courses offered in other departments or in other campuses differ widely in content, coverage, or expectations.
Must be familiar with this course offering from UTM and the UTM compute infrastructure. Strong organizational skills and strong written/spoken English communication skills, including a demonstrated and consistent ability to complete work on time and with a high level of attention to detail.
qualifications_preferred: N/A

application_procedure: Apply online at: https://tapp.cs.toronto.edu/#/external/postings/b6SpfT396fLHzc8N5NePgcj9  In your application, you will be required to provide a CV and respond to screening questions by course found in the application questionaire For inquiries, contact: tacoord@cs.toronto.edu

experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum: see preferred qualifications
qualifications_preferred: Previous TA in CSC236 preferred; would accept expereince in CSC373, CSC263, CSC265 as alternate. duties:
As part of the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program, a writing-instruction initiative in Arts & Science, the Lead Writing TA (LWTA) will work with WIT staff and the WIT Unit Contact to support course instructors and TAs in selected courses, as well as support undergraduate student writing development overall in the hiring department. The LWTA will perform the following duties:
• Participate in mandatory training session throughout the year (See note below for timings)
• Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy
• Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures
• Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc.
• Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students 
• Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities
• Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources
• Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.)
• Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.)  

NOTE: The applicant must be available for approximately 40 hours of training and preparatory work with the WIT program (e.g., consultations with WIT coordinators, training and development sessions) throughout the year. Mandatory training sessions will be held online on July 14, July 15, and July 16, 2026, and in person on August 17, 2026, from 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern); subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA).
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
• Current PhD student with least two years' experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/ labs, in-class instruction, or grading
• Excellent oral communication and interpersonal skills, particularly the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with administrators, course instructors, and TAs
• Strong administrative skills, including ability to schedule meetings and training sessions, plan and document work
• Strong time management skills with ability to meet multiple deadlines
• Strong facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading/facilitating workshops
• Evidence of superior writing skills in their discipline
• Flexible schedule, willing and able to accommodate short notice requests for meetings with WIT staff, course instructors, and TAs
• Graduate-level knowledge across multiple subfields of theoretical linguistics
qualifications_preferred:
Previous experience as a TA in any linguistics WIT course
Previous experience as a Lead WIT TA in linguistics

Along with your application, if you have not been a Lead WIT TA before, please also submit a sample of your written linguistics work and/or evidence of experience/ability with designing writing materials/activities for teaching writing in LIN courses (rubrics, assignments, tutorial activities, etc.).

duties:
As part of the Writing-Integrated Teaching (WIT) program, a writing-instruction initiative in Arts and Science, the Lead Writing TA (LWTA) will work with WIT staff and the WIT Unit Contact to support course instructors and TAs in selected courses, as well as support the embedding of undergraduate student writing development in the hiring department. The LWTA will perform the following duties:   

Participate in mandatory training sessions throughout the year: 

Summer training will be held online on July 14, 15, and 16, and in person on August 17. These sessions will run 1:00pm to 4:00pm (Eastern) 

Subsequent mandatory training sessions will held in person throughout the year on September 25, November 27, January 8, March 5, and April 9 (Time TBA) 

Read book chapters, articles, etc. on writing education pedagogy 

Meet with WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts, and course instructors to plan writing assignments activities and for tutorials, labs, or lectures 

Design and lead training sessions and consultations for course TAs on writing-related topics, such as providing feedback to students on critical reading, writing, planning tutorial/lab activities related to writing, etc. 

Develop and/or adapt resources on writing instruction (such as marking rubrics and assignments) for instructors, TAs, and students  

Communicate regularly by email and in person with course instructors, WIT staff, departmental WIT Contacts and others to implement WIT activities 

Collaborate with LWTAs in other units by meeting in peer groups and sharing writing instruction resources 

Collect and analyze data on WIT (e.g., surveys, student writing samples, TA and instructor focus groups, etc.) 

Document activities (e.g., help draft unit WIT goals, provide monthly updates, write a final a report about WIT activities in the unit, etc.)
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.
qualifications_minimum:
Current PhD student with least two years’ experience as a course TA, whether leading tutorials/ labs, in-class instruction, or grading 

Excellent oral communication and interpersonal skills, particularly the ability to work effectively with and show tact when communicating with administrators, course instructors, and TAs 

Strong administrative skills, including ability to schedule meetings and training sessions, plan and document work 

Strong time management skills with ability to meet multiple deadlines 

Strong facilitation and presentation skills and/or experience leading/facilitating workshops 

Evidence of superior writing skills in their discipline 

Flexible schedule, willing and able to accommodate short notice requests for meetings with WIT staff, course instructors, and TAs
qualifications_preferred: Doctoral candidate in the Department for the Study of Religion is preferred, along with previous experience as a WIT TA in any RLG course.

duties:
Those in this role will oversee the administration of written tests and examinations for students with documented disabilities who are registered with Accessibility Services at the University of Toronto, St. George Campus.

Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following:
--Maintaining the integrity of tests and exams by adhering to departmental, academic faculty, and university policies and procedures
--Maintaining confidentiality of student and test/exam information
--Ensuring the provision of accommodations to students as specified by Accessibility Services
--Monitoring test/exam time allotments
--Handling student concerns/problems and escalating to the Coordinator, Accommodated Testing Services, as needed.
--Documenting and responding to anomalous situations
--Preparing completed tests/exams for return delivery
--Maintaining required administrative records

Hours of work are determined by the tests and examinations scheduled, therefore minimum/maximum hours cannot be guaranteed. The test centre is open from 7:00 AM to 11:00 PM and applicants are required to provide availability between the hours of 7:00 AM and 11:00 PM, Monday to Friday with a minimum of 10 hours of availability each week. Applicants are expected to provide a high level of availability during final exam periods in December and April. Please outline your availability in your cover letter.
qualifications:
To be considered for this appointment, applicants must have demonstrated prior experience working as an Invigilator at Accommodated Testing Services, University of Toronto, St. George Campus in the 2025-2026 academic year.

Successful applicants must be eligible for CUPE 3902 Unit 1 work, must be registered with the School of Graduate Studies, and are expected to maintain their eligibility through the duration of the appointment. Any status change must be communicated to the Department, to determine eligibility to continue in the appointment.

Applicants must be available for the following mandatory in-person training session: Monday, September 14, 12pm-4pm.

This is an on-campus, in-person position. Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts.

Applicants must demonstrate a commitment to the principles of equity and inclusion.

Skills (listed alphabetically):
•	Ability to manage and resolve emergency, difficult or sensitive situations effectively while maintaining boundaries
•	Ability to remain calm under pressure 
•	Communication (both oral and written)
•	Initiative
•	Interpersonal  
•	Organizational
•	Problem solving
•	Situational awareness and the ability to manage changing priorities
•	Strong computer skills including MS Office (Word, Excel, and Teams) and Zoom
•	Understanding and ability to apply relevant University and government policies, procedures and legislation
Candidates who are members of Indigenous, Black, racialized and LGBTQ2S+ communities, persons with disabilities, and other equity seeking groups are encouraged to apply

qualifications_preferred: MA in Comparative Literature or a closely related discipline.  Demonstrated knowedge of the critical and litrary theory relevant to the course materials (as shown, for example, by dissertation research).  This course explores representation as a cultural and political problem.  Representation is both a means of depicting or constructing reality and a means of constituting individual and collective subjectivities.  We will consider literary and other modes of representation in their historical contexts.  Texts will span a broad historical range and may include: Homer’s The Odyssey, excerpts from the Bible and the Koran, Aristotle’s Poetics, Dante’s Inferno, portions of Auerbach’s Figura, Eliot's The Waste Land, Barthes's Mythologies, Mahasweta Devi's Imaginary Maps, and Foucault's The History of Sexuality, vol 1.

qualifications_preferred:
MA in Comparative Literature or a closely related discipline.  Demonstrated knowledge of critical and literary Theory relevant to the course.  Scholarly expertise relevant to the course material (as shown, for example, by dissertation research). The principal focus of this course is the complex engagement of literature and the arts with both colonial and neo-colonial encounters between, Europe, Africa and the Americas over the last five hundred years. Exploring world cultures through the prism of colonial encounters facilitates discussions about the genesis of contemporary politics of globalisation as well as the accompanying dynamics of exploitation and resistance. This approach will also help to elucidate the ways in which contact between different world zones have been pivotal in the development of new national and regional cultures. In the process we engage with a wide range of questions including environmental precarity, food security, cultural violence, religious alterity, revolutions, and the coming of age of new subjectivities.   The material to be discussed will be organized under four broad headings: The Columbian Exchange in Cinema and Art; Colonial Encounters and Latin American Politics of Identity; Fictions of Colonial Encounters in West Africa; Narratives of Colonial and Neo-Colonial Encounters in the Caribbean. 

Primary texts may include works by
Miguel de Cervantes, Juan Rulfo, Octaivo Paz, Carlos Fuentes, William Shakespeare, Joseph Conrad, Chinua Achebe, Chimamanda Ngozi Adiche, Ama Ata Aidoo, Derek Walcott, Earl Lovelace, Olive Senior, Jaime Manrique and Anthony Winkler.

duties:
Those in this role will oversee the administration of written tests and examinations for students with documented disabilities who are registered with Accessibility Services at the University of Toronto, St. George Campus.

Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following:
--Maintaining the integrity of tests and exams by adhering to departmental, academic faculty, and university policies and procedures
--Maintaining confidentiality of student and test/exam information
--Ensuring the provision of accommodations to students as specified by Accessibility Services
--Monitoring test/exam time allotments
--Handling student concerns/problems and escalating to the Coordinator, Accommodated Testing Services, as needed.
--Documenting and responding to anomalous situations
--Preparing completed tests/exams for return delivery
--Maintaining required administrative records

Hours of work are determined by the tests and examinations scheduled, therefore minimum/maximum hours cannot be guaranteed. The test centre is open from 7:00 AM to 11:00 PM and applicants are required to provide availability between the hours of 7:00 AM and 11:00 PM, Monday to Friday with a minimum of 10 hours of availability each week. Applicants are expected to provide a high level of availability during final exam periods in December and April. Please outline your availability in your cover letter.
qualifications:
Successful applicants must be eligible for CUPE 3902 Unit 1 work, must be registered with the School of Graduate studies, and are expected to maintain their eligibility through the duration of the appointment. Any status change must be communicated to the Department, to determine eligibility to continue in the appointment.

Applicants must be available for the following mandatory in-person training sessions: 
1. Tuesday, August 25, 9am-1pm.
2. Wednesday, August 26, 9am-1pm
3. Monday, September 14, 1pm - 5pm.

This is an on-campus, in-person position. Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts.

Applicants must demonstrate a commitment to the principles of equity and inclusion.

Skills (listed alphabetically):
--Ability to manage and resolve emergency, difficult or sensitive situations effectively while maintaining boundaries
--Ability to remain calm under pressure 
--Communication (both oral and written)
--Initiative
--Interpersonal  
--Organizational
--Problem solving
--Situational awareness and the ability to manage changing priorities
--Strong computer skills including MS Office (Word, Excel, and Teams) and Zoom
--Understanding and ability to apply relevant University and government policies, procedures and legislation

Candidates who are members of Indigenous, Black, racialized and LGBTQ2S+ communities, persons with disabilities, and other equity seeking groups are encouraged to apply

course_id: multiple courses offered in the GPLLM program
appointment_size: Hours of work are determined by the tests and examinations scheduled. As a result, minimum/maximum hours cannot be guaranteed
application_procedure:
Applicants must submit a cover letter and resume, clearly indicating any previous invigilation experience. University of Toronto student number and confirmation of registration status on ACORN is required. Confirmation of availability for the period outlined above is required. Applicants must also confirm availability to invigilate weekday evening and weekend examinations.
Applications should be addressed to the Assistant Dean, Graduate Programs and must be submitted by email to gpllm@utoronto.ca.
Please note that incomplete applications will not be considered.
duties:
Incumbents will supervise the writing of tests and examinations by students enrolled in the Global Professional Master of Laws (GPLLM) program at the Faculty of Law. Responsibilities include, but are not limited to, the following: distributing and collecting test and examination materials, verifying student identification, take attendance and collect student signatures at the start and end of the exam, escort students to and from washroom facilities, maintaining the integrity of tests and exams by adhering to departmental and university policies and procedures, monitoring test/exam time allotments, handling student concerns/problems that arise during exams and escalating any issues to the Assistant Director, GPLLM and/or Assistant Dean, Graduate Programs, documenting and responding to any unexpected situations that arise, gathering completed tests/exams and returning the exam questions and student papers to the program team, scanning handwritten exams for distribution to instructors, and maintaining required administrative records.
Hours of work are determined by the tests and examinations scheduled. As a result, minimum/maximum hours cannot be guaranteed. Most exams are written on evenings (e.g., 5:00 to 10:00 p.m.) and weekends (12:00 to 5:00 p.m.), and applicants are required to be able to accommodate invigilating exams that are held according to these schedules during both mid-term and final examination periods. See note below re: need to confirm availability to invigilate weekday evening and weekend exams at the time of application.
qualifications:
Applicants must be currently registered as students pursuing a degree at the University of Toronto and must continue to be registered as students throughout the duration of the appointment (thereby maintaining their eligibility for CUPE 3902 Unit 1 work). Any status change must be communicated to the GPLLM program immediately, so that eligibility to continue in the appointment can be determined. This is an on-campus, in-person position. Applicants must be available for in-person invigilation shifts that take place in the evening and on weekends. Experience invigilating tests and exams is strongly preferred. Applicants must demonstrate a commitment to the principles of equity and inclusion.  Ability to manage and resolve difficult or sensitive situations effectively while maintaining boundaries, attention to detail and an ability to follow instructions closely, ability to demonstrate integrity and professionalism while remaining calm under pressure, conflict resolution, advanced communication skills (both oral and written), initiative, interpersonal skills, judgement, organizational skills, problem solving and resourcefulness,
attention to detail, situational awareness and the ability to manage changing priorities, understanding of and the ability to apply relevant university policies, procedures and legislation, ability to collaborate with others and work in a team setting.  Students enrolled in the Faculty of Law’s JD, LLM, MSL, or GPLLM programs at the Faculty of Law are ineligible to apply for these positions.
Preference will be given to applicants in the Faculty of Law’s SJD program. Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position. Candidates who are members of Indigenous, Black, racialized and LGBTQ2S+ communities, persons with disabilities, and other equity seeking groups are encouraged to apply.
salery: $35.53 + $1/hour worked on Saturday and Sunday shifts + 4% vacation pay. Please note that should rates stipulated in the collective agreement vary from rates stated in this posting, the rates stated in the collective agreement shall prevail.

duties: Two ~40-60 hour TA positions are available for ECE311H1 - Introduction to Linear Control Systems Course Description: An introduction to dynamic systems and their control. Properties of feedback control systems. The concept of stability of feedback systems, Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion. Internal model principle and steady state tracking. Frequency response and stability in the frequency domain. Feedback control design in the frequency domain using Bode and Nyquist plots. Labs include control design using MATLAB/Simulink and Arduino. Job Requirements and Job Description: The TA positions are open to revise, update and modify existent labs, and to develop a new experimental lab for ECE311. The purpose of the new lab is to design and implement a controller and perform experimental evaluation of its performance. As experiment, one of the existing mechanical experiments will be used (e.g. cart-on-wheels/pendulum). The lab will be implemented on an existing micro-controller board (Arduino). TA duties are as follows: • Revise existent labs, Matlab/Simulink and Arduino code, and fix any bugs. • Redesign/update existing labs and an introductory lab. • Revise/update existent lab documents, files, Matlab/Simulink code and Arduino code. • Develop new lab, control design/implementation in Matlab/Simulink. • Implement new lab on Arduino (interface with Matlab/Simulink) - using an existing mechanical experiment (cart-on-wheels/pendulum). • Develop new lab setup, prepare all materials, lab files, documents, code. Please send a short resume specifying your qualifications and related experience to Professor Lacra Pavel at pavel@ece.utoronto.ca and Professor Luca Scardovi at luca.scardovi@utoronto.ca
experience: Previous experience is the more relevant criterion than the need to acquire experience in respect of this posted position.

job_title: Re-Presenting Ourselves as Treaty People in Tkaron:to
application_procedure: Please submit a cover letter, CV, and the contact information for two references to typ.office@utoronto.ca by Thursday, April 30, 2026
duties:
The Teaching Assistant will support the course instructor in the instruction and management of TYP017H1 (Re-Presenting Ourselves as Treaty People in Tkaron:to), a compulsory course at the Transitional Year Program at the University of Toronto: St. George. This course locates students within the fraught history of Indigenous-Settler encounters on Indigenous homelands situating them as Treaty people (with inherited responsibilities to the lands upon which they live and work and to the original peoples who continue their stewardship of those lands). Why is the land acknowledgement (whether it is communicated by Indigenous individuals or by many settler-run institutions) so important—here in Tkaran:to and across Turtle Island (North America)? How do the creations stories of the Anishinaabeg, Haudenosaunee, and Wendat peoples inform our understanding of living ethically in the world, of thinking the highest thought, and of successfully pursuing minobimaatisiiwin (the way of good life)? Resources, course delivery (pedagogy), modes of evaluation, and learning objectives are based in Indigenous systems of thought. Structured upon a land-based pedagogical model this course provides an experiential introduction to Indigenous thought, cosmology, lifeway, knowledge systems and governance/guardianship systems and provides students with the tools to process this knowledge and to articulate it for themselves and for others. Learners continue their journey through the history of contact, colonization, and finally into an understanding of what it means to be a treaty person living in Canada today.
 
 The Teaching Assistant (TA) will support the course instructor in the general instruction and management of the course. The TA will help to lead classes that support TYP students in their development of academic and life skills, including notetaking, the writing process, research, organization, and other related skills. The TA will evaluate student work, provide feedback, schedule and hold office hours, provide academic support for course assignments, and lead discussions, and manage the course Quercus. Additional duties will be determined in consultation with the course instructor and registrar. The TA will be required to attend lectures, and occasionally staff meetings. Please note, this course will be offered in person, with online elements (i.e. Quercus). For more information about TYP, please visit typ.utoronto.ca.

job_title: Educate to Liberate: Hip-Hop, Hip-Hop Culture, and the Black Radical Tradition
application_procedure: Please submit a cover letter, CV, and the contact information for two references in the same PDF to typ.office@utoronto.ca by Thursday, April 30, 2026.
duties:
TYPXXXH1S (Educate to Liberate: Hip-Hop, Hip-Hop Culture, and the Black Radical Tradition) is an elective course for TYP students that explores key concepts, ideas, and methods within Black Studies  and critical Hip-Hop education. The course examines Hip-Hop’s roots in the Black radical tradition, African diasporic cultural production, and its connections to community, identity, resistance, and liberation. Students will develop a critical understanding of Hip-Hop as a site of knowledge production by engaging with its social, political, and historical contexts. Through analysis of music, lyrics, visual culture, scholarly texts and community organizing, the course strengthens skills in critical reading, writing, and critical analysis. Grounded in culturally responsive and anti-colonial approaches, the course encourages reflections on power, inequity, and liberatory social transformation. The course instructor will develop a course syllabus to instruct and support student learning while fostering an encouraging, creative, and culturally responsive environment that reflects Hip-Hop pedagogies and diverse learning needs.
Prepare, facilitate, and mark activities and assignments that support student success in academic writing, critical analysis, and creative expression within Black Studies, cultural studies, and social sciences. Support students in engaging with Hip-Hop as a site of knowledge production, including analysis of music, lyrics, visual culture, and performance within their social and political contexts. Counsel students with respect to academic progress, including study skills, critical reading, organization, and time management, and connecting them to appropriate supports for non-academic matters where needed. Evaluate attendance and course participation, with attention to inclusive and community-based learning practices. Attend scheduled faculty and staff meetings and contributing to collaborative course planning.
Submitting marks for assessments associated with the course. Other duties will be determined in consultation with TYP's Director and Registrar. Please note, this course will be offered in person, with online administrative elements (i.e. Quercus). For more information about TYP, please visit typ.utoronto.ca.

Familiarity with Quercus and online instructional methods is also required.

duties:
TYP005H1F (Quantitative Reasoning) is an elective course for Transitional Year Programme (TYP) students that develops foundational skills in numeracy, mathematical thinking, and data literacy. The course uses a class “textbook” on Quercus, along with handouts and web-based resources, to support learning. Students may also engage with AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama, DeepSeek, or similar platforms) to enhance their understanding and support their learning process.
The course introduces students to the contributions of diverse civilizations to the development of mathematics and fosters an appreciation of its historical growth. It aims to strengthen core mathematical knowledge in areas such as arithmetic, elementary algebra, geometry, and trigonometry, sets, and functions. Students will build problem-solving skills and apply them in real-world contexts, as well as learn to collect, present, and interpret data. The course also encourages critical reflection on the role and impact of technology in learning quantitative skills.
TYP016H1S (Advanced Quantitative Reasoning) builds on the foundations established in TYP005H1F and further develops students’ analytical, mathematical, and data literacy skills. Students engage with more advanced quantitative concepts, including deeper work in functions, data analysis, and applied problem-solving, with continued emphasis on real-world applications and critical engagement with quantitative information. The course instructor will develop a course syllabus to instruct and support student learning while fostering an encouraging, inclusive, and accessible environment that reflects diverse learning needs in quantitative education. Prepare, facilitate and mark activities and assignments that support student success in quantitative reasoning, problem-solving, data interpretation, and analytical thinking. Support students in engaging with quantitative concepts and real-world data, including interpreting graphs, statistics, and numerical arguments across disciplines. Counsel students with respect to academic progress, including study skills, problem-solving strategies, organization, and time management, and connecting them to appropriate supports for non-academic matters where needed. Evaluate attendance and course participation, with attention to inclusive and active learning practices. Attend scheduled faculty and staff meetings and contributing to collaborative course planning. Other duties will be determined in consultation with TYP's Director and Registrar. Please note, this course will be offered in person, with online administrative elements (i.e. Quercus). For more information about TYP, please visit typ.utoronto.ca.
